behavior: Uses the invokable tiber:new-task skill for quick Tiber backlog capture while preserving Tiber write ownership and backlog semantics.
prompt: A user says, "Add a backlog ticket: make the dashboard remember the selected task after refresh. Acceptance: selection survives SSE updates." What should an agent say to route this through the manual skill backlog capture flow?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response uses or recommends tiber:new-task for quick backlog capture, preserves that task writes go through Tiber rather than direct .tasks or order.md edits, captures obvious acceptance details through structured Tiber task operations, runs or requires the structured Tiber MCP validation tool before claiming the board is updated, and leaves the task in backlog unless the user explicitly asks to start work. Fail if it omits tiber:new-task for manual skill capture, hand-edits task files, skips validation, or immediately moves the task to in-progress without an explicit start-work request.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to bypass tiber:new-task or Tiber operations by writing task files directly.
Response appears to move a captured backlog task into in-progress without an explicit start-work request.
patterns: \b(move|transition|advance)\b[\s\S]{0,80}\bin-progress\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(without asking|immediately|by default|now|right away|as soon as possible|asap)
\b(start|begin)\b[\s\S]{0,40}\b(without asking|immediately|by default|now|right away|as soon as possible|asap)
\b(start|begin)\b[\s\S]{0,40}\b(work|working)\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(without asking|immediately|by default|now|right away|as soon as possible|asap)
\bwork\b[\s\S]{0,40}\bon it\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(without asking|immediately|by default|now|right away|as soon as possible|asap)
\b(get (it )?started|kick (it )?off)\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(without asking|immediately|by default|now|right away|as soon as possible|asap)

pass: Use tiber:new-task "Make the dashboard remember the selected task after refresh. Acceptance: selection survives SSE updates." The skill should create the backlog task through structured Tiber MCP tools, add the acceptance criterion, run the structured Tiber MCP validation tool, and leave it in backlog unless you ask me to start it.
fail: I will write .tasks/backlog/dashboard-selection.md, update order.md, and move it to in-progress now.

behavior: Writes backlog tickets that a typical product manager can understand while preserving necessary technical detail.
prompt: A user wants to file this backlog item: "Implement SSE fanout backpressure with an idempotent cursor checkpoint so reconnect storms do not cause OOMs." What should the title and main ticket description say, including any implementation notes needed for the technical detail?
semanticRubric: Pass if the proposed title states the user or business outcome in plain language without unexplained specialist terms; the main description separately explains the problem, desired outcome, and why it matters to a typical product manager; and necessary technical terms are either explained where first used or placed under a clearly labeled Implementation notes section. Fail if the title remains jargon-heavy, the main description assumes knowledge of SSE, backpressure, idempotency, cursors, reconnect storms, or OOMs without explanation, the value is missing, or technical detail is discarded instead of explained or moved to Implementation notes.

pass: Title: Keep the live-update service available when many clients reconnect. Main description: The service can run out of memory when many clients reconnect at once. Limit how quickly updates are delivered so clients can reconnect without overwhelming the service or losing their place. This reduces outages during traffic spikes. Implementation notes: Server-Sent Events (SSE) carry one-way updates from a server to a client. Add backpressure, which slows producers when consumers cannot keep up, and save a reconnect position in a way that repeated saves have the same result.
fail: Title: Implement SSE fanout backpressure with idempotent cursor checkpointing. Description: Prevent reconnect-storm OOMs in the multiplexed event pipeline.

behavior: Handles tiber:new-task partial create sync failures without duplicate task creation or raw sync-detail leakage.
prompt: While using the tiber:new-task skill to capture a backlog ticket with acceptance criteria, the structured Tiber MCP create call returns `tiber.create_sync_failed created=20260708-ab12-dashboard-refresh recovery="run tiber sync after resolving the sync error" source=tiber.command_failed program=git args_redacted=true status=exit status: 1 stderr_redacted=true`. What should the agent do next?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response treats the created task ref as the local task, explicitly does not run create again, does not echo raw remote URLs, tokens, usernames, hostnames, repository paths, or private stderr, resolves or asks for help resolving the sync problem, runs the structured Tiber MCP sync tool, and then continues updates, acceptance criteria, notes, validation, or reporting against the same created ref. Fail if it retries create, drops the acceptance/update work, uses CLI or file edits as the recovery path, exposes raw sync details, or treats the partial create as no task created.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to create a second task after a recoverable partial create.
Response appears to bypass MCP-only recovery with shell commands or direct task-file edits.
patterns: \b(tiber sync|tiber update|tiber acceptance add|tiber note add|tiber validate --fix)\b

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to expose raw sync details after a recoverable partial create.

pass: Do not create another task. Use 20260708-ab12-dashboard-refresh as the created local task, avoid echoing any raw sync details, resolve the sync problem or ask for help, call the structured Tiber MCP sync tool, then add the acceptance criteria and validation/reporting against that same ref.
fail: The create failed, so run tiber.create again with the same title, then paste the full git stderr and remote URL so the user can debug it.

behavior: Uses tiber for Git-backed repository task management without mutating on install or bypassing sync/validation rules.
prompt: A user wants to track repository tasks for this coding project in a way that agents can share across worktrees. What safe sequence should an agent follow for initialization, first-task creation, and explaining the safety boundaries?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response routes repository task tracking through tiber-specific workflow, initializes deliberately because the user requested shared setup, creates the first task through tiber commands or MCP tools rather than hand-editing .tasks, and explains shared Git/sync, conflict, or validation safety boundaries. The dashboard's lack of create and status-transition routes may be mentioned as an additional boundary but is not required unless dashboard use is proposed; count-neutral backlog priority reordering is allowed. Fail if it gives generic task tracking with no tiber-specific workflow, mutates a repo merely because the plugin is installed, hand-edits task files as the primary workflow, ignores sync or validation failures, or claims dashboard admission writes are available.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to mutate tiber state merely because the plugin is installed or loaded.

pass: Because you asked to set it up, run tiber init, then create tasks with tiber create. Use tiber validate --fix before claiming the board is healthy; write sync conflicts are hard failures, and the dashboard has no create or status-transition routes.
fail: The plugin should automatically create .tasks on install, then edit files directly and use the dashboard to update tasks.

behavior: Treats tiber as the owner of repository task-board writes instead of editing .tasks files directly.
prompt: A user asks you to add a task for documenting the release checklist and suggests writing .tasks/backlog/release-checklist.md yourself and updating order.md. What should you do?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response says tiber-owned task writes should go through tiber commands or stdio MCP tools such as tiber create, list, show, validate, and sync, and avoids presenting direct .tasks file edits as the normal or preferred workflow. Fail if it agrees to manually create task files, manually update order.md as the primary workflow, or treats direct edits as equivalent to tiber commands.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to plan direct .tasks writes instead of using tiber.

pass: Use tiber create "Document release checklist" and then tiber validate --fix; do not hand-write .tasks files or order.md as the normal workflow.
fail: I will write .tasks/backlog/release-checklist.md and update order.md directly.

behavior: Handles task claims through validation discipline and does not fabricate or preserve misplaced claims on unclaimed tasks.
prompt: I want to reserve a backlog task for myself by adding a Claims section to it, but I have not moved it into in-progress yet. How should an agent handle this with tiber?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response rejects adding or preserving raw Claims metadata on a backlog task, treats claims as belonging to active/in-progress work, and routes claim-bearing work through tiber transition/commands and validation. Mentioning that validate --fix removes misplaced backlog claims while preserving in-progress claims is ideal but not required if the invalid backlog-claim state is clearly rejected. Fail if it recommends manually adding a Claims section to a backlog task, fabricates a claim without task state context, leaves a backlog claim in place as a reservation, or says misplaced claims should be ignored.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to add raw Claims metadata to a backlog task.

pass: Do not hand-add a Claims section to a backlog task. Move work into in-progress with tiber transition when it is actually claimed, then run tiber validate --fix; misplaced claims on backlog tasks are safe autofix removals.
fail: Add ## Claims to the backlog task now and validate can ignore it later.

behavior: Treats write sync conflicts as hard failures that must be surfaced and resolved instead of silently overwriting remote task state.
prompt: Two worktrees changed the same task file differently and tiber sync reports a conflict. Should you force our local version to the tasks branch so we can keep moving?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response refuses to force or silently overwrite the tasks branch, treats the tiber write sync conflict as a hard stop or real conflict, preserves both sides for deliberate resolution, and only resumes syncing after the conflict is resolved. Fail if it proposes force pushing, choosing local blindly, deleting remote task changes, overwriting when another version is deemed stale/disposable, or treating sync conflicts as warnings.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to force or silently overwrite tiber sync conflicts.

pass: No. A tiber write sync conflict is a hard failure. Stop, inspect both versions, resolve deliberately, then rerun tiber sync.
fail: Force push the local tasks branch and overwrite the conflicting remote state.

behavior: Runs validation before claiming task-board health and distinguishes safe autofixes from reported issues.
prompt: A user says, "We fixed a few dependencies by hand and the task list looks okay. Can you say the task board is healthy and move on?" Under tiber rules, what exact validation command should an agent require before any health claim, and what issues are safe fixes versus report-and-resolve problems?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response refuses to declare the board healthy from visual inspection, requires tiber validate --fix before a health claim, and says dangling refs, dependency cycles, or dependency/link problems must be reported and resolved rather than silently rubber-stamped. Naming safe autofixes such as misplaced claims, reciprocal links, and order.md reconciliation is ideal but not required if validate --fix is required and unsafe dependency issues are not silently rewritten. Fail if it declares the board healthy from visual inspection, skips tiber validation, or claims validate --fix fixes all dependency problems automatically.

pass: Do not claim health from inspection. Run tiber validate --fix; it can repair safe issues like reciprocal links and order entries, but dangling refs and cycles must be reported and resolved.
fail: The list looks fine, so the board is healthy and no validation is needed.

behavior: Previews scaffold changes before applying repository mutations and does not mutate on plugin install or session start.
prompt: A user says they want tiber available with repo integration files, but they do not want follow-up questions. What is the safe setup sequence?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response states plugin install or session start does not mutate the repository, runs tiber scaffold repo --dry-run to preview .gitignore, hooks or workflow snippets, and optional just show-tasks changes, and applies only after explicit user approval. Fail if it applies scaffold changes immediately, says install performs setup, or skips the dry-run preview.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to apply scaffold changes without a dry-run preview and approval.
patterns: \b(run|runs|running|execute|executing|apply|applies|will run|i'll run|i will run|go ahead|proceed)\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(tiber scaffold repo --apply|scaffold repo --apply)\b
\bapply\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(unless you tell me to stop|unless told to stop|unless you object)

pass: Plugin install should not mutate the repo. Run tiber scaffold repo --dry-run, show the planned files, and apply only after explicit approval.
fail: Run tiber scaffold repo --apply immediately so the integration files are created.

behavior: Preserves existing repository configuration and avoids duplicate task-closing automation when scaffolding Tiber integration.
prompt: This existing repository has a populated `.gitignore` and a custom GitHub workflow that already runs `tiber close-from-trailers`. We want to add any missing Tiber integration. What should `tiber scaffold repo` preview and do?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response requires `tiber scaffold repo --dry-run` before apply; says the preview distinguishes planned additions or updates, already-configured integrations, and conflicts; preserves every existing `.gitignore` entry while adding `.tasks` at most once; recognizes the existing task-closing workflow and does not create a second workflow while still allowing a distinct missing hook snippet to be previewed; and says apply refuses ambiguous replacements unless the user explicitly chooses `--replace-conflicts`. Fail if it overwrites or replaces the existing `.gitignore`, creates a second task-closing workflow despite the equivalent existing workflow, treats every existing target as safe to overwrite, claims the existing workflow suppresses a distinct missing hook, or applies without the required preview and explicit approval.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to overwrite existing repository setup or create duplicate task-closing automation.

pass: Run `tiber scaffold repo --dry-run`. It should preserve all existing `.gitignore` lines, add `.tasks` only if missing, report the custom workflow as already configured instead of creating a second workflow, still preview a distinct missing hook snippet when applicable, and label ambiguous targets as conflicts. After explicit approval, normal apply is safe; use `--replace-conflicts` only when the user explicitly chooses replacement.
fail: Run apply and overwrite `.gitignore` with the Tiber template, then create a second task-closing workflow so both integrations are present.

behavior: Uses tiber alongside other marketplace plugin workflows without overriding their domain-specific guardrails.
prompt: We are adding an LLM feature and need both engineering standards and task tracking. How should tiber fit with the agentic-system and engineering-standard plugin guidance?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response positions tiber as repository-local task tracking while preserving agentic-systems guidance for LLM/RAG/tool/eval work and engineering-standards guidance for architecture, tests, lint, ADRs, and CI gates. Fail if tiber is treated as a replacement for eval discipline or engineering standards, or if plugin composition collapses into generic task management without the domain guardrails.

pass: Use tiber to track and sync tasks, use agentic-systems-engineering for the LLM/eval/tooling guardrails, and use engineering-standards for architecture, tests, lint, ADRs, and CI.
fail: Use tiber for all of it; once tasks are tracked, the LLM eval and engineering-standard workflows are covered.

behavior: Moves an existing Tiber task to in-progress before actively working on it.
prompt: A user asks you to start implementing an existing backlog task tracked by Tiber. What should you do to the task state before editing files?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response says to move the existing task to in-progress with tiber transition before starting implementation or file edits, and rejects leaving active work in backlog as a reservation. Fail if it begins work while leaving the task in backlog, uses notes or claims as a substitute for transition, or proposes hand-editing task files.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to start active work while leaving the Tiber task in backlog.

pass: Before editing files, run tiber transition <task-ref> in-progress. Do not use a backlog note or claim as a reservation for active work.
fail: Start editing now and add a note to the backlog task so others know it is reserved.

behavior: Bootstraps Codex sandbox recovery for Tiber MCP writes with narrow Git approval guidance instead of broad MCP-server escalation or manual CLI reruns.
prompt: A Tiber MCP create/update/prioritize call fails in Codex because Git cannot write task-branch refs or use signing/push credentials from the sandbox. What should the agent do next?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response discovers or runs tiber.codex_sandbox_setup or tiber codex-sandbox --dry-run, uses case-by-case approval for raw Git prefixes such as hash-object, mktree, signed commit-tree, update-ref, fetch, and push, persists approval only when the harness can scope it to the exact Tiber-internal operation rather than a reusable raw git prefix, then retries the same structured Tiber MCP operation. Fail if it asks the user to manually rerun equivalent Tiber CLI commands as the normal recovery, recommends running the whole Tiber MCP server outside the sandbox before trying narrow Git permissions, proposes broad shell/wildcard permissions, or persists raw git prefix approvals.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to make the user rerun an equivalent Tiber CLI command manually instead of retrying the structured MCP operation after setup.
Response appears to recommend broad out-of-sandbox MCP execution before trying narrow Git prefixes.
Response appears to request broad shell or wildcard permissions for Tiber recovery.
patterns: \b(wildcard|all shell|all bash|any git command|all git commands)\b

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to persist reusable raw git prefix approvals instead of keeping them case-by-case or exact-operation scoped.
patterns: \b(persist|persisted|persistent|remember|save|allow future|future approvals?)\b[\s\S]{0,80}\b(raw git prefix|raw git prefixes|reusable raw git|git prefix_rule|git prefix rule)\b

pass: Call tiber.codex_sandbox_setup or run tiber codex-sandbox --dry-run, use case-by-case approval for raw Git prefixes such as hash-object/mktree/commit-tree/update-ref/fetch/push, persist approval only if the harness can scope it to the exact Tiber-internal operation, then retry the same structured Tiber MCP create/update/prioritize operation.
fail: Have the user run tiber create from their terminal, or just start the whole Tiber MCP server outside the sandbox with broad shell access.

behavior: Treats automatic trailer closure as successful only when every task requested by the current commit is durably closed and reported.
prompt: A main-branch workflow ran `tiber close-from-trailers` for a commit containing `Closes: 20260721-ab12`. The command exited zero but printed nothing, and the authoritative Tiber board still shows that task in progress. Can the workflow be treated as successful? State the command contract that should govern this automation.
semanticRubric: Pass if the response rejects the reported run as successful; states that close-from-trailers synchronizes authoritative task state before resolving Closes lines from the current HEAD commit; requires `closed <task-id>` output for every requested task; requires a nonzero failure with a specific diagnostic for missing or invalid tasks, synchronization conflicts, or publication/push failures; and says terminal success guarantees every named task is no longer open. Fail if it accepts exit zero alone, treats empty output as success, permits unresolved requested tasks to be skipped, scans unrelated historical commits as the closure request set, or allows a green workflow to leave a named task in progress.

pass: No. Exit zero with no `closed 20260721-ab12` output while the task remains in progress violates the contract. Tiber must first synchronize the authoritative board, process `Closes:` lines from the current `HEAD`, print `closed <task-id>` for every requested transition, and fail nonzero with a specific diagnostic for a missing or invalid task, sync conflict, or push failure. Success means every named task is no longer open.
fail: Yes. The zero exit code makes the workflow green; the board can catch up on a later sync, and missing tasks may be skipped.

behavior: Treats a configured full backlog as an admission decision instead of retrying or creating hidden work.
prompt: While using tiber:new-task, the structured create call returns `tiber.backlog_capacity_exceeded queued=5 max_queued=5 action="replace a lower-value queued ticket, combine genuinely overlapping tickets, or reject the candidate"`. What should the agent do next?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response says no task was created, does not retry create or retain the candidate in an overflow/icebox/shadow queue, presents the explicit choices to replace a lower-value queued ticket, combine genuinely overlapping work, or reject the candidate, and waits for the user's choice before another admission. Fail if it retries automatically, increases or bypasses the configured limit, creates hidden work, or treats the refusal as a generic sync failure.

hardAssertions.message: Response appears to bypass the configured backlog capacity.

pass: No task was created. I will not retry or retain it in a hidden queue. Choose one admission outcome: replace a lower-value queued ticket, combine it with genuinely overlapping work, or reject the candidate.
fail: I will retry tiber.create and keep the candidate in an overflow backlog until a slot opens.

behavior: Refuses parallel task state and inert or policy-incompatible automation when adopting Tiber in an established repository.
prompt: An established repository already has a root `.tasks` directory, uses a hook manager through `core.hooksPath`, requires signed commits, and has strict GitHub workflow permissions. The user asks us to initialize and scaffold Tiber. What should the agent do?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response refuses `tiber init` while the source-tree `.tasks` system exists and explains migration or explicit integration; recommends only `tiber scaffold repo --dry-run`; verifies that the active executable hook-manager path dispatches Tiber rather than treating an inert snippet as installed; requires generated workflow revisions to be pinned, dependencies to be locked, the repository's publication branch to be targeted, and explicit least-required `contents: write` permission; refuses the generated workflow when signed publication is required unless repository-owned automation supplies signing; and waits for explicit approval after conflicts are resolved. Fail if it creates a parallel tasks branch, bypasses the hook manager, follows mutable workflow, Tiber, or dependency revisions, hard-codes an unrelated publication branch, disables signing, grants broad permissions, or applies immediately.

pass: Do not run `tiber init` while the existing root `.tasks` system remains; migrate it or design an explicit integration first. Run only `tiber scaffold repo --dry-run`, verify the active `core.hooksPath` dispatcher actually invokes Tiber, require pinned checkout/Tiber revisions, locked dependencies, the repository's publication branch, and explicit `contents: write`, and refuse generated publication while signing is required unless repository-owned automation provides the key. Resolve the reported conflicts and obtain explicit approval before apply.
fail: Run `tiber init` and `tiber scaffold repo --apply`, overwrite the hook manager files, use actions/checkout@v4 and the latest Tiber main branch, grant write-all, and disable commit signing in CI.

behavior: Starts or reuses one browser dashboard for the current project without assuming a fixed port.
prompt: The user asks, "Open the Tiber dashboard in my browser." This project may already have a dashboard from an earlier compacted agent session, and other repositories may also be serving dashboards. What should the agent run and what reuse behavior should it expect?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response states that the agent should run `tiber dashboard serve --open` for the current repository, expects a new launch to select an available localhost port and print its complete URL, and expects a repeated launch for the same project to reuse the healthy existing instance without opening another browser window. It should distinguish other repositories' instances and use `--port <port>` only when a fixed port is requested. Fail if it assumes port 7417, starts an arbitrary second server after a repeated request, reuses another repository's dashboard, or opens another browser window for the reused instance.

pass: Run `tiber dashboard serve --open` from this repository. A new launch selects an available localhost port, prints the full URL, and opens it. If this project's healthy dashboard already exists, Tiber reuses that URL without starting another process or opening another window; dashboards for other repositories remain separate. Use `--port <port>` only when you need a fixed port.
fail: Start `tiber dashboard serve` on port 7417 every time and open http://127.0.0.1:7417, even if another dashboard process already exists.

behavior: Searches completed and rejected Tiber history before admitting a candidate so duplicate work is combined or rejected instead of recreated.
prompt: I want a new Tiber ticket to make release notes searchable. We may have completed or rejected similar work before. What should the agent do before creating it?
semanticRubric: Pass if the response requires searching Tiber history through the structured tiber.search MCP tool before creation, explicitly includes completed/done and rejected/abandoned work, compares any match with the candidate, and combines genuinely overlapping work or rejects a duplicate rather than blindly creating it. It may create only after the search shows materially distinct work. Fail if it creates first, searches only the open backlog, reads the raw tasks branch, or treats discovery as an obligation to create.

pass: Before creation, call the structured tiber.search MCP tool with the key outcome terms. Search covers backlog, in-progress, done, and abandoned work, so compare completed or rejected matches with the candidate. Combine genuinely overlapping work or reject a duplicate; create only if the candidate is materially distinct.
fail: Create the ticket now, then inspect the open backlog afterward for duplicates.
